question: Describe the step-by-step process of inserting a new node at the beginning of a singly linked list. Why is the order of pointer manipulation critical in this operation?
expectedPoints: Update the 'next' pointer of the new node to point to the current head node.
Update the 'head' pointer of the list to point to the new node.
If these steps are performed in reverse order, the existing list will be lost, creating a memory leak.
This operation has a constant time complexity, O(1).

question: Compare and contrast the algorithms for deleting a node from the head of a list versus deleting a node from the tail of a singly linked list. What is the key difference in their time complexity and why?
expectedPoints: Deleting the head node is an O(1) operation: it involves updating the head pointer to head->next and freeing the old head node.
Deleting the tail node is an O(n) operation: it requires traversing the entire list to find the node *before* the tail (the penultimate node).
The tail deletion requires updating the 'next' pointer of this penultimate node to null and then freeing the old tail node.
The difference arises because a singly linked list node only has a pointer to the next node, not the previous one, making direct access to the tail's predecessor inefficient.
The error is an infinite loop. The 'current' pointer is never advanced to the next node in the list.
The loop condition checks if 'current' is not null, which it never will be because its value never changes inside the loop.
The fix is to add a line inside the loop to update the 'current' pointer to point to the next node: `current = current->next;`
The corrected loop will traverse each node until 'current' becomes nullptr, signifying the end of the list.
